video_title: Are The Rich Starting To Stumble? | Danielle DiMartino Booth
gist: Danielle DiMartino Booth argues the US already ran through a labor-market recession in 2025 masked by K-shaped averages, and the key new risk is that even top-decile earners' confidence and spending are finally cracking. She expects negative headline CPI prints, no 2026 rate hike (against consensus), a clearing commercial-real-estate/private-credit bust, and advises harvesting gains, holding cash at ~4%, and stepping back into precious metals.

themes.theme: Hidden Labor-Market Recession Beneath the Averages

insights.claim: The Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (the hard data underlying non-farm payrolls, covering 95%+ of US employers) showed net job losses in Q1, Q2 and Q3 of 2025, which meets and exceeds the definition of a recession.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
entities: Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages
insights.so_what: The 'resilient' headline economy masks a labor-market recession already recorded in the most reliable data.
insights.quote: that from US employers told us that in the first, second, and third quarters of 2025, there were net job losses. So that that goes beyond the definition of a recession.

insights.claim: The US economy lost roughly 600,000 full-time jobs on a rolling 12-month basis, with recent job creation skewed toward part-time and temporary leisure/hospitality roles tied to the World Cup.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Headline job gains are low-quality and temporary, overstating true labor demand.
insights.quote: We have a lot of leisure and hospitality jobs created because of the World Cup. Um, but they're part-time in nature and they're temporary in nature

insights.claim: Investors only ever trade off the first non-farm payroll print, so subsequent revisions to hard data are effectively irrelevant to market participants even when they reveal recessionary job losses.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A real economic downturn can persist in the data without moving markets, delaying repricing.
insights.quote: the audience is investors are only ever going to trade off of the first non-farm payroll print. What happens subsequent to that is completely irrelevant to their day jobs.

insights.claim: Despite bonus-depreciation incentives in the One Big Beautiful Bill, companies outside AI are spending on rebuilding and over-stocking inventory (defensively) rather than on capex/building things (offensively), which the bill was supposed to stimulate.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: The promised capex-driven growth boost is not showing up outside AI, undercutting bullish GDP forecasts.
insights.quote: Companies are spending money, but they're spending money on inventory... They're spending it defensively versus offensively

insights.claim: Companies initially deferred capex because of the Iran war and missile risk, then held off further amid threats of fresh tariffs, driving panic buying of inventory ahead of feared cost spikes rather than durable investment.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Geopolitical and trade uncertainty is diverting corporate cash from productive investment into precautionary stockpiling.
insights.quote: Initially, they were holding off because missiles went flying and because of the Iran war.

insights.claim: The One Big Beautiful Bill's promised average tax-refund boost of $1,000+ actually came in around $326 and was more than consumed by higher gas prices before those prices later fell.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: The fiscal boost to household spending power was far smaller than advertised and was eaten by energy costs.
insights.quote: at the margin a delta of $1,000 or more on average with tax refunds. That ended up being about $326 that was more than consumed by higher gas prices

themes.theme: Rising Bankruptcy Cycle and Consumer Fragility

insights.claim: Bankruptcies are running about 40% year-over-year higher and QI Research's proprietary PPI for bankruptcy attorneys looks like a hockey stick, indicating an accelerating insolvency cycle.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A steep rise in insolvencies signals deeper stress than headline growth suggests.
insights.quote: bankruptcies running about 40% um year-over-year above where they were... it's a hockey stick.

insights.claim: The corporate bankruptcy wave of 2023-2024 is now feeding a lagged rise in consumer bankruptcies, up about 10% year-over-year as failing companies lay off workers and liquidate rather than secure bridge financing.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: The 'caboose' of consumer distress is now arriving, pressuring household spending over coming quarters.
insights.quote: now we're seeing the caboose. So now we're seeing consumer bankruptcies are up about 10% year-over-year.

insights.claim: As of July 1, all 42 million Americans with student loans (about one in six borrowers coming off forbearance in place since March 2020) become obligated to repay, creating a payment shock that is contributing to rising bankruptcies.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A large, delayed debt-servicing obligation is hitting consumers just as cushions are thinning.
insights.quote: we finally have all 42 million Americans obligated to repay their student loans. And so there'll be this kind of kicker price shock

insights.claim: Consumers keep spending regardless, piling gigantic sums onto revolving credit with buy-now-pay-later 'humming along in the background', so credit stress alone won't stop consumption.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Watch confidence and wealth effects, not credit usage, for the signal that spending finally rolls over.
insights.quote: The American consumer never stops spending... we've seen consumer credit just go haywire.

themes.theme: The Top of the K Starts to Crack

insights.claim: Within both Conference Board and University of Michigan surveys, it is the top income earners whose confidence is now falling most quickly, an inversion of the usual pattern where high earners are impervious.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: If the top leg of the K weakens, the averages that have propped up the economy could break.
insights.quote: it's those top earners whose confidence is coming down the most quickly.

insights.claim: Per BEA/Fed flow-of-funds decile data, only the 80-90th percentile cohort still has a small cash cushion, while nearly all remaining cash sits with the top 90-100% decile.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Spending power is extremely concentrated, so a hit to the very top has outsized macro consequences.
insights.quote: the 80 to 90%... they're the only cohort that has like a little bit of a cash cushion left and then all of the other cash is sitting with that 90 to 100% top desile.

insights.claim: Carnival Cruise Lines missed revenue last week, a red flag for baby-boomer spending because boomers dominate cruise demand and such a miss hasn't occurred since the pandemic.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A discretionary-spend miss among affluent retirees is an early tell that top-tier consumption is softening.
insights.quote: Carnival Cruise Lines just told us last week that whoops, we missed our revenue. Um that to me is a red flag for baby boomers

insights.claim: Americans over age 70 now own about a third of the US stock market and seniors hold their highest-ever equity exposure, so a 20-25% market correction would trigger a severe negative wealth effect that freezes their spending.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Boomer net worth propping up the economy is fragile and highly correlated to a single equity-market drawdown.
insights.quote: people over the age of 70 now own a third of the US stock market

insights.claim: Median retirement savings for the average boomer is a little under $250,000, far too little to last 20 years, especially while supporting adult children, so the widely cited $90 trillion in boomer net worth is extremely unevenly distributed.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: The 'boomer wealth backstop' narrative overstates broad household resilience because the wealth sits with a thin top slice.
insights.quote: The median retirement savings for the average boomer is like a little under 250,000... It's not going to last you 20 years.

themes.theme: Disinflation, the Fed and Kevin Warsh

insights.claim: DiMartino Booth predicts headline CPI will soon print negative month-over-month, driven by falling oil, home-price declines spreading beyond the Sun Belt, services disinflation and wage growth back to 2019 levels on the Atlanta Fed tracker.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Imminent negative CPI prints would upend the market's rate-hike narrative and reprice rate expectations.
insights.quote: we're going to see negative prints on headline CPI. It's just it's going to happen.

insights.claim: She expects no Fed rate hike in 2026, explicitly betting against the market's consensus that pricing a first hike around September, and shared that other guests interviewed hold the same view.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A contrarian no-hike (potentially cut) call versus a hawkish market sets up a large repricing if correct.
insights.quote: I don't think we're going to see a rate hike this year, Adam.

insights.claim: New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh is deliberately killing forward guidance, cut the FOMC statement to its shortest since Greenspan, may skip press conferences, and is running Fed communications far quieter than the Powell era.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Less Fed communication removes a key market crutch and raises volatility around data surprises.
insights.quote: chopped the FOM statement in half... he even went so far as to say, we might not even have press conferences if we don't have anything to say.

insights.claim: Warsh signaled at his first press conference that non-farm payroll data is useless until the third revision (the QCEW), which DiMartino Booth reads as him tacitly acknowledging the economy was in recession.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
entities: Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages
insights.so_what: The Fed chair himself may privately accept the recession thesis, shaping policy toward easing.
insights.quote: he mentioned that payrolls data were only relevant on their third revision... That means he knows that the economy was in recession.

insights.claim: Corporate margins are being squeezed because companies cannot pass along higher input costs (visible in the CPI-vs-PPI gap), with Dallas Fed survey respondents urgently pleading for lower interest rates.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Margin compression amid disinflation strengthens the case for rate cuts and threatens earnings.
insights.quote: companies have said unequivocally and if you look at the difference between CPI and PPI, we can't pass along the higher prices.

insights.claim: Logistics data show transportation costs rising over March-May while unit transportation volumes fell 13.3% over the same span, a divergence hitting margins hard per the Logistics Managers Index.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Rising costs on falling volumes is a direct margin drag and a sign of weakening real activity.
insights.quote: Transportation costs have increased over the March to May period while unit transportation volumes have are down 13.3% over that same two-month span.

themes.theme: CRE Clearing, Shadow Banking and Private Credit Risk

insights.claim: The long-awaited commercial real estate downturn is now in full swing with 'extend and pretend' over, distressed office sales at a 10-year high and stress bleeding into multifamily.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A clearing CRE bust pressures bank lending standards and liquidity precisely as consumer cushions thin.
insights.quote: the commercial real estate cycle that we've been waiting for forever is finally in fullblown... Extend and pretend is bye-bye.

insights.claim: The Fed's balance sheet is growing again (perhaps $50-60 billion a month of overnight-market operations), which DiMartino Booth argues is de facto QE unlike prior 'not-QE' episodes when the balance sheet was shrinking.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Renewed balance-sheet growth suggests hidden liquidity stress and quietly supports asset prices.
insights.quote: unlike the prior episodes of it not being QE when the Fed's balance sheet was shrinking, now the Fed's balance sheet is growing again.

insights.claim: The shadow/non-bank financial system has for two years been about 51-52% of global assets (roughly $258 trillion as of end-2024), larger than the regulated banking system and effectively unregulated.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A majority of global assets sits outside regulation with opaque valuations, a systemic tail risk.
insights.quote: 258 trillion as of the end of 2024. 51% of global assets unregulated in the shadows larger than what we consider to be out in the open

insights.claim: DiMartino Booth rates her concern about private markets a 7 or 8 out of 10, reasoning that visible bankruptcies are up 40% year-over-year so unseen insolvencies and valuations in the non-bank system are likely worse, amplified by higher-for-longer rates.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Opaque private-market valuations are a leading candidate for the next credit shock.
insights.quote: I would put that up at a seven or an eight given the publicly visible bankruptcy cycle being 40% higher year-over-year.

insights.claim: A feedback loop exists whereby public companies value their private holdings at questionable levels, so if roughly half of Mag-7-type earnings rest on recurring 'one-time' accounting marks, both the E and the P/E could be inflated.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Questionable private marks may be quietly propping up public equity earnings and valuations.
insights.quote: So what if the E and the PE ratio because public companies are valuing their private holdings at questionable levels? There is a feedback loop somewhere in there

insights.claim: The push to sell private-market assets to retirement funds and retail investors is, in DiMartino Booth's read, largely an effort to offload junk, echoing the 1990s DLJ-era creation of private-equity retail funds by lowering minimums to $250,000.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Retail democratization of private assets may transfer risk from sophisticated sellers to unsophisticated buyers at the top.
insights.quote: I watched this marketing created in its first iteration. Um, and I knew why it was happening then. So, I think we definitely know why it's happening now.

insights.claim: Blue Owl now trades as effectively a penny stock, illustrating how public-market repricing is exposing weakness in private-credit sponsors.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Publicly listed private-credit proxies are already signaling deep stress in the space.
insights.quote: Blue Owl is a penny stock right now, right?

themes.theme: Speculative Excess and the AI Capex Cliff

insights.claim: A record $251 billion of equity was sold in the first half of the year, with the biggest players selling as much debt and equity as possible because the issuance window has effectively closed.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Record insider/corporate selling into strength is a classic late-cycle distribution signal.
insights.quote: we've had $251 billion dollars of equity sold in the first half of the year, breaking all prior records.

insights.claim: AI and AI-related companies now make up roughly 45% of the S&P 500's market cap, meaning the broad index (not just the Nasdaq) is far more concentrated and exposed than during the 1999-2000 bubble.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Even index and thematic investors are heavily exposed to a single AI capex theme, raising systemic drawdown risk.
insights.quote: AI and AI related companies are like 45% of the S&P market cap right now. Not the NASDAQ... but the S&P

insights.claim: Adam Taggart argues the biggest catalyst for a market correction would be anything that compromises AI capex expectations (projected ~$800B this year, over $1T next), citing physical constraints like capacitors, permitted land, power, skilled engineers and copper, plus local data-center moratoriums.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: A shortfall or slowdown in the AI buildout could be the trigger that forces a broad market repricing.
insights.quote: the biggest contender for what delivers the wakeup call... it's going to be something that compromises expectations in that capex buildout.

insights.claim: The reported postponement of OpenAI's IPO to 2027 and OpenAI pulling its IPO were, in DiMartino Booth's view, meaningful red flags that redefined 'first mover advantage', yet markets shrugged them off within a day.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Delayed marquee AI IPOs hint the funding window is closing, but complacent markets are ignoring the signal.
insights.quote: When the New York Times reported that OpenAI was postponing its IPO until 2027, I'm like, hello.

insights.claim: Speculation is at extremes with margin debt at all-time highs, 11 leveraged/inverse ETFs trading the day after the SpaceX IPO, and retail investors now driving markets as their share of trading has doubled over 15 years.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Peak speculative behavior led by leveraged retail flows marks late-cycle froth vulnerable to a break.
insights.quote: There were 11 trading the day after SpaceX IPOed. 11.

insights.claim: Retail investors control an outsized share of the market but 'sling around little actual money', trading options on leveraged ETFs on margin, so their dominance is highly leveraged and fragile.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Market leadership resting on thin, highly leveraged retail positions can unwind violently.
insights.quote: they're buying options on leverage ETFs on Mar... it's ridiculous that they control so much of the market, but yet they're throwing around so little actual money.

themes.theme: Positioning: Harvest Gains, Cash, Gold and the Midterms

insights.claim: DiMartino Booth says for the first time in a long time it is time to step back into precious metals, arguing much of gold's consolidation is behind us and speculative 'tourists' have been shaken out.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A tactical re-entry signal on gold from a macro bear, tied to easing inflation fears and a potential no-hike Fed.
insights.quote: I just said last week for the first time in a long time, I think it's time to step back in.

insights.claim: One of the easiest trades right now is locking in about 4.1% on a two-year Treasury, effectively getting paid to hold cash while waiting for opportunities.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Attractive risk-free yield makes patience cheap and supports a defensive posture.
insights.quote: you can get 4.1% on a two-year. I mean, just go lock that in. Lock and load.

insights.claim: Her core strategy is 'let your profits ride, but never let it be said that you lost a penny' — harvest gains over time so you end up playing with house money, and hedge rather than chase risk.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A disciplined gain-harvesting and hedging framework for late-cycle markets where flows still dominate.
insights.quote: let your profits ride, but never let it be said that you lost a penny.

insights.claim: Recent rotations out of tech into defensives are month/quarter-end rebalancing and flows, not genuine changes of mind, so 'you cannot fight the flows' but should be hedged.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Don't over-interpret rotation headlines as regime change; structural flows still rule until they don't.
insights.quote: No, we're just rebalancing. It's the end of the month. It's the end of the quarter. It's just flows, people.

insights.claim: The midterms will matter to markets, which currently believe headlines; a Democratic blue-wave sweep of House and Senate would prompt markets to price in universal basic income and bypassing the banking system to pay people directly, while a split Congress (House flip, GOP Senate) is the market-preferred gridlock outcome.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Election outcomes could reprice fiscal-policy expectations well ahead of the vote, by around Labor Day.
insights.quote: I think a sweep would have people like me beginning to impute universal basic income and bypassing the banking system, giving money directly to people.

insights.claim: A Wall Street Journal-profiled investor liquidated his private-credit holdings early after a weekend of reflection, taking out an $80 million trade and leaving clients with large gains but no locked-up capital, illustrating the value of pre-emptively exiting illiquid private credit before 'redemption caps' (modern gates) appear.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Liquidity, not just returns, matters — exiting private credit early avoids being trapped when redemptions are capped.
insights.quote: gates go up. They don't call them gates anymore. Now they say refund redemptions have been capped.
